name: Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop
name_original: La Silla de Felipe II – running circular por el Parque de La Herrería
description: This difficult 3.6-mile jogging route through Parque de La Herrería offers panoramic views from La Silla de Felipe II and historic intrigue.
region_intro: Jogging around San Lorenzo De El Escorial offers varied terrain within the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains, at an average altitude of over 1,000 meters. The region is characterized by dense pine forests, notably around Mount Abantos, and features ash and oak forests in areas like La Herrería. Runners can expect mountainous landscapes with significant elevation changes, providing challenging and rewarding experiences. Best jogging routes around San Lorenzo De El Escorial The most popular jogging route is Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop, a 3.6 miles (5.8 km) trail that takes about 44 minutes to complete. This route offers panoramic views of the Monastery and the La Herrería forest, combining scenic beauty with historical context. Another top favourite among local runners is Mount Abantos – View of Monte Abantos loop from Zaburdón, a difficult 11.3 miles (18.2 km) path. This demanding route ascends Mount Abantos, offering significant elevation changes and rewarding views. Local runners also love the Abantos – Mount Abantos loop from San Lorenzo de El Escorial, a 5.7 miles (9.1 km) trail leading through the pine forests of Mount Abantos, often completed in about 1 hour 45 minutes. Jogging around San Lorenzo De El Escorial is defined by mountainous terrain, dense pine forests, and historic estates like La Herrería. The network of routes offers options for various ability levels, from scenic loops to challenging ascents. The routes in San Lorenzo De El Escorial are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 8 reviews. More than 600 runners have used komoot to explore San Lorenzo De El Escorial's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: There are over 30 running routes around San Lorenzo de El Escorial, catering to various fitness levels. While many routes involve significant elevation changes due to the mountainous terrain, you can find options ranging from easy loops to challenging ascents.

Q: Are there easy jogging routes suitable for beginners in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: Yes, while San Lorenzo de El Escorial is known for its mountainous terrain, there are a few easier jogging routes available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957916232/seat-of-philip-ii-and-la-herreria-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a more moderate experience, combining scenic views with a manageable distance.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on the running trails?
A: Jogging routes in San Lorenzo de El Escorial are characterized by stunning natural features. You'll frequently run through dense pine forests, especially around Mount Abantos, and encounter ash and oak forests in areas like La Herrería. The elevated position of the town also means panoramic views of the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains are common.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the jogging routes?
A: Absolutely! Many routes offer historical and scenic points of interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/740607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II&lt;/a&gt; is a famous granite viewpoint offering panoramic views of the Royal Monastery and the La Herrería forest. You might also pass by the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2505177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mount Abantos&lt;/a&gt; itself, or even catch glimpses of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4318799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Monastery of San Lorenzo de El Escorial&lt;/a&gt; on some town-adjacent routes.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: The running routes in San Lorenzo de El Escorial are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Runners often praise the challenging mountainous terrain, the rewarding views, and the opportunity to explore dense pine forests and historic estates like La Herrería.

Q: Are there any long-distance or advanced running routes for experienced runners?
A: Yes, San Lorenzo de El Escorial is an excellent destination for experienced runners seeking a challenge. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23824936" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mount Abantos – View of Monte Abantos loop from Zaburdón&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 18 km with significant elevation, are considered difficult and provide rigorous training opportunities. The region even hosts a half marathon known for its substantial elevation changes.

Q: Can I find circular jogging routes in the area?
A: Many of the running routes around San Lorenzo de El Escorial are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957916232/seat-of-philip-ii-and-la-herreria-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the more demanding &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33932443" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abantos – Mount Abantos loop from San Lorenzo de El Escorial&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are the running trails in San Lorenzo de El Escorial dog-friendly?
A: Generally, the natural trails and forests around San Lorenzo de El Escorial are suitable for running with dogs, provided they are well-behaved and kept under control, especially in protected areas. Always ensure you follow local regulations regarding leashes and waste disposal to preserve the natural environment.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: Due to its elevated position in the Sierra de Guadarrama, San Lorenzo de El Escorial offers pleasant conditions for jogging during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summer mornings and evenings can also be enjoyable. Winters can be cold, and higher elevation routes may experience snow, requiring appropriate gear and caution.

Q: Are there any lakes or reservoirs near the running routes?
A: Yes, the region features several beautiful water bodies. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5155198" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent feature nearby, and you might encounter smaller lakes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6385961" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Gato&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes offer views of these serene landscapes, adding to the scenic beauty of your run.

Q: Are there any family-friendly jogging paths in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: For families looking for a more relaxed run or stroll, areas like the Parque de La Herrería offer gentler paths. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957916232/seat-of-philip-ii-and-la-herreria-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seat of Philip II and La Herrería Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt;, while having some elevation, is a popular choice that combines natural beauty with historical interest, making it enjoyable for various ages.

Q: Where can I find parking for the running trails?
A: Parking is generally available in and around San Lorenzo de El Escorial, particularly near popular trailheads and attractions like La Herrería. Specific parking information can often be found on individual route descriptions or local signage. It's advisable to check for designated parking areas, especially during peak seasons.
tips:
From this natural observatory the monarch contemplated the development of the works of the Monastery.
Some experts assure that these granite seats, for the king and two companions, are the remains of a thousand-year-old Celtiberian altar. From here you can perceive that the Monastery has the shape of an inverted grill, in homage to San Lorenzo, who was martyred in one of them back in the year 258, since it is located on a small hill from which the Guadarrama Valley and the Sierra de Navacerrada.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail for La Silla de Felipe II?
faqs.answer: There are parking areas available near the Parque de La Herrería, which provide access to the trail. Some parking spots offer easier access directly to La Silla de Felipe II, while others might require a longer, steeper hike from the base of the hill.

faqs.question: Is this running route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: While the Komoot platform rates this specific jogging route as 'difficult' due to its length and elevation, many sections of the trail through Parque de La Herrería are considered fairly easy with accessible paths. However, some parts require sure-footedness, and the full experience can be challenging. If you're a beginner, be prepared for varied terrain and some inclines.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, offering a mix of experiences. You'll encounter paved, unpaved, natural paths, and even some asphalt sections. There are also specific 'mountain hiking path' and 'alpine' sections, as well as stepped platforms carved out of granite, particularly around the Seat of Philip II.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is beautiful year-round, but spring is particularly lovely with blooming wildflowers. The natural surroundings, including lime, maple, chestnut, and Pyrenean oak trees, offer pleasant scenery in various seasons. For cooler temperatures, consider spring or autumn. On clear days, you might even see the skyscrapers of Madrid in the distance.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the La Silla de Felipe II trail?
faqs.answer: The Parque de La Herrería is a protected natural space. While dogs are generally allowed, it's always best to keep them on a leash to protect the local wildlife and flora, and to ensure a pleasant experience for all visitors.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to access Parque de La Herrería?
faqs.answer: Parque de La Herrería is a protected natural space and part of the Natura 2000 Network. Generally, there are no entrance fees or permits required for public access to the park itself for recreational activities like jogging. However, always check local regulations for any specific events or restricted areas.

faqs.question: What historical or natural highlights can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The main highlight is the Seat of Philip II, a granite formation with stunning panoramic views. The park itself, Bosque de la Herrería, boasts varied flora including Pyrenean oak, wild pear, and cherry trees, and is excellent for birdwatching. You can also spot remnants of a Roman road.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with several other official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the "De Valencia a Lisboa (Etapa desde San Lorenzo del Escorial a Pelayos de la Presa)", "Carretera de la Fuente de la Reina", and various segments of "Camino de las Arenillas", among others.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of La Silla de Felipe II?
faqs.answer: Tradition holds that King Philip II used this granite formation, the Seat of Philip II, to oversee the construction of the Royal Monastery of San Lorenzo de El Escorial. More recent research suggests it might have been a pre-Roman Vetton altar, adding a fascinating layer of ancient history to the site.

faqs.question: Is the trail crowded, and when is the best time to avoid crowds?
faqs.answer: While generally peaceful, the trail, particularly around the Seat of Philip II, can get quite crowded, especially on weekends. To enjoy a more tranquil experience, consider visiting on weekdays or early in the morning.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter in Parque de La Herrería?
faqs.answer: The Bosque de la Herrería is a rich natural environment. While specific wildlife sightings can vary, the area is known for its diverse birdlife, making it a good spot for birdwatching. You'll also be surrounded by a variety of trees, shrubs, and ferns.
